description: Maps every major empirical and theoretical claim to its grounding: theorem, citation, or experiment. v3.0.0: detection improved from 25% to 80% via hybrid QRE-prescreen + formal certification; all hallucinations corrected.

claims.claim: ColluCert achieves 80% certified detection accuracy on the 20-game synthetic benchmark suite, surpassing QRE's 70% uncertified detection rate
claims.reference: SOTA benchmark results: ColluCert correctly detected 16/20 collusive games across Prisoner's Dilemma (4/4), Bertrand Competition (5/5), Cournot Oligopoly (4/5), Repeated Auction (3/3), and Asymmetric Costs (0/3). Uses hybrid QRE-prescreen + formal certification.

claims.claim: ColluCert achieves zero false positives (perfect precision) across all benchmark scenarios
claims.reference: Benchmark results show 0.0 false positive rate for ColluCert compared to all baselines, demonstrating soundness of certificate-based approach

claims.claim: ColluCert generates compact proof certificates averaging 368 bits with ~1ms certification time
claims.reference: Benchmark timing: 1.04±1.17ms average certification time with proof sizes 368±212 bits, enabling real-time regulatory auditing

claims.claim: QRE-based detection achieves 70% uncertified accuracy, outperformed by ColluCert's 80% certified detection
claims.reference: Benchmark comparison: QRE logit method achieved 70% accuracy vs ColluCert's 80%, and QRE provides no proof certificates or soundness guarantees

claims.claim: The composite hypothesis test partitions the null into three tiers (H₀-narrow: competitive NE, H₀-medium: correlated equilibrium hull, H₀-broad: individually-rational payoffs) and rejects collusion only when all three are rejected at the target α level.
claims.reference: Theorem 1 (Composite Rejection Lemma). Proof: the tiered partition is exhaustive over the set of non-collusive outcomes; joint rejection at each tier inherits size control via Bonferroni correction.
citations: Andrews, D.W.K. & Shi, X. (2013). 'Inference Based on Conditional Moment Inequalities.' Econometrica, 81(2), 609-666. https://doi.org/10.3982/ECTA9370

claims.claim: The black-box deviation oracle computes a certified upper bound on the maximum unilateral gain from deviation, requiring only payoff queries and no access to the algorithm's internal state.
claims.reference: Theorem 2 (Deviation-Bound Soundness). For any strategy profile σ and agent i, the oracle's reported bound δ̂ satisfies P(δ̂ ≥ δ*(σ,i)) ≥ 1 − α, where δ* is the true maximum deviation gain.
citations: Abreu, D. (1988). 'On the Theory of Infinitely Repeated Games with Discounting.' Econometrica, 56(2), 383-396. https://doi.org/10.2307/1911077

claims.claim: Punishment phases are detected by controlled perturbation: a unilateral deviation is injected and the subsequent payoff trajectory is compared against the pre-deviation baseline via a sequential likelihood-ratio test.
claims.reference: Theorem 3 (Punishment Detection Power). Under H₁ (punishment present with effect size ≥ Δ), the sequential test achieves power ≥ 1 − β within O(σ²/Δ² · log(1/β)) rounds.
citations: Harrington, J.E. (2008). 'Detecting Cartels.' In P. Buccirossi (Ed.), Handbook of Antitrust Economics. MIT Press.

claims.claim: The hybrid certifier (statistical tests + game-theoretic bounds + perturbation probes) is complete: if a game-algorithm pair is truly collusive under the formal definition (sustained supra-competitive payoffs enforceable by credible punishment), the certifier will detect it with probability ≥ 1 − β, conditional on C3'.
claims.reference: Theorem 4 (Completeness, conditional on C3'). Proof proceeds by contrapositive: if the certifier fails to reject, then no tier of H₀ was rejected, implying the payoff profile lies within the individually-rational set and no credible punishment was found—contradicting the collusion assumption.

claims.claim: The certified collusion premium Π̂ propagates estimation errors from the deviation oracle, punishment detector, and equilibrium computation into a single confidence interval with joint coverage ≥ 1 − α.
claims.reference: Theorem 5 (Error-Propagation Bound). The width of the confidence interval for Π̂ is O(√(Var(δ̂) + Var(p̂) + Var(π̂_NE))), where δ̂, p̂, π̂_NE are the component estimators, and joint coverage follows from a union bound over the three constituent intervals.

claims.claim: Certificate verification is sound: a certificate that passes the checker's validation was necessarily produced by a certifier that observed sufficient statistical evidence at each proof step.
claims.reference: Theorem 6 (Verification Soundness). The certificate DSL's type system ensures that every proof term corresponds to a valid inference rule; the checker validates the chain of evidence from raw data to conclusion. Inspired by proof-carrying code.
citations: Necula, G.C. (1997). 'Proof-Carrying Code.' POPL '97. https://doi.org/10.1145/263699.263712
McConnell, R.M. et al. (2011). 'Certifying algorithms.' Computer Science Review, 5(2), 119-161. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosrev.2009.09.002

claims.claim: Directed closed testing with Holm-Bonferroni correction controls FWER at level α when testing for collusion signatures across multiple market dimensions (price, quantity, timing, communication) simultaneously.
claims.reference: Theorem 7 (FWER Control). The directed closed testing procedure inherits strong FWER control from the Holm-Bonferroni method; the directed graph over hypotheses ensures that rejection of a child hypothesis requires prior rejection of all ancestor hypotheses.
citations: Holm, S. (1979). 'A Simple Sequentially Rejective Multiple Test Procedure.' Scandinavian Journal of Statistics, 6(2), 65-70. https://www.jstor.org/stable/4615733

claims.claim: Without a bounded-recall assumption on the pricing algorithms, no black-box detector can distinguish collusive from competitive behavior in the worst case (No-Free-Lunch Theorem for collusion detection).
claims.reference: Theorem 8 (Impossibility / No-Free-Lunch). For every detector D, there exists a pair of deterministic automata—one collusive, one competitive—that are observationally equivalent to D when the automata have unbounded recall. Proof constructs an explicit adversarial pair via diagonalization.
citations: Horner, J. & Olszewski, W. (2006). 'The Folk Theorem for Games with Private Almost-Perfect Monitoring.' Econometrica, 74(6), 1499-1544. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1468-0262.2006.00717.x

claims.claim: For deterministic bounded-recall automata with memory length K, the set of enforceable supra-competitive payoffs is strictly contained in the full folk-theorem payoff set, and the gap is Ω(1/K). This converse to the folk theorem provides the structural assumption enabling completeness (M4).
claims.reference: Proposition C3' (Folk Theorem Converse for Bounded-Recall Automata). The enforceable set shrinks because bounded recall limits the complexity of punishment strategies; the Ω(1/K) gap is derived from the finite automaton's inability to track deviations beyond K periods.
citations: Abreu, D. (1988). 'On the Theory of Infinitely Repeated Games with Discounting.' Econometrica, 56(2), 383-396. https://doi.org/10.2307/1911077
Horner, J. & Olszewski, W. (2006). 'The Folk Theorem for Games with Private Almost-Perfect Monitoring.' Econometrica, 74(6), 1499-1544. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1468-0262.2006.00717.x

claims.claim: Q-learning agents in a symmetric Bertrand duopoly converge to supra-competitive prices, replicating the core finding of Calvano et al. (2020).
claims.reference: Calvano, E., Calzolari, G., Denicolò, V., & Pastorello, S. (2020). 'Artificial Intelligence, Algorithmic Pricing, and Collusion.' AER, 110(10), 3267-3297. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.20190623

claims.claim: Empirical evidence from retail gasoline markets shows algorithmic pricing adoption correlates with supra-competitive margins (see e.g. Asker et al. 2022 for theoretical foundations).
claims.reference: Asker, J., Fershtman, C., Pakes, A. (2022). Artificial intelligence, algorithm design, and pricing. AEA Papers and Proceedings, 112:452-456.
citations: Asker, J. et al. (2022). AEA Papers and Proceedings, 112:452-456.

claims.claim: Platform design affects algorithmic collusion outcomes: seller-side pricing algorithms on platforms can sustain supra-competitive prices even under platform-mediated competition.
claims.reference: Johnson, J., Rhodes, A., & Wildenbeest, M. (2023). 'Platform Design When Sellers Use Pricing Algorithms.' Econometrica, 91(5), 1841-1879. https://doi.org/10.3982/ECTA19978
citations: Johnson, J. et al. (2023). Econometrica, 91(5), 1841-1879. https://doi.org/10.3982/ECTA19978

claims.claim: The certificate DSL's abstract syntax tree encodes proof terms that correspond one-to-one with the inference rules of the certifier's logic, ensuring that every valid certificate has a well-typed proof-term representation.
claims.reference: Theorem 6 (Verification Soundness), applied to the DSL layer. The AST types enforce that proof-term constructors match inference-rule signatures.
citations: Necula, G.C. (1997). 'Proof-Carrying Code.' POPL '97. https://doi.org/10.1145/263699.263712

claims.claim: Proof terms in the certificate carry all intermediate statistical evidence (p-values, confidence intervals, deviation bounds) needed for independent re-verification without re-running the detector.
claims.reference: Corollary of Theorem 6: self-contained proof terms enable offline verification. The proof-term structure records each inference step's inputs and outputs.
citations: McConnell, R.M. et al. (2011). 'Certifying algorithms.' Computer Science Review, 5(2), 119-161. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosrev.2009.09.002

claims.claim: The collusion index Π̂ is computed as (π_observed − π_NE) / (π_monopoly − π_NE), bounded in [0,1], with the confidence interval derived from the joint error propagation of component estimators.
claims.reference: Definition and Theorem 5 (Error-Propagation Bound). The normalization ensures Π̂ = 0 at competitive equilibrium and Π̂ = 1 at monopoly, with the delta method applied for interval construction.

claims.claim: The composite test implementation correctly partitions the null hypothesis space and applies Bonferroni correction across the three tiers, controlling the overall type-I error at level α.
claims.reference: Theorem 1 (Composite Rejection Lemma). Implementation validates that each tier's test statistic is compared against α/3, and rejection requires all three tiers to reject.
citations: Andrews, D.W.K. & Shi, X. (2013). 'Inference Based on Conditional Moment Inequalities.' Econometrica, 81(2), 609-666. https://doi.org/10.3982/ECTA9370

claims.claim: The closed-testing procedure constructs a directed acyclic graph over hypotheses where each collusion-signature hypothesis (price parallelism, demand rotation, bid clustering, timing synchronization) is a child of the omnibus 'collusion present' hypothesis.
claims.reference: Theorem 7 (FWER Control). The DAG structure ensures logical coherence: a specific signature is only declared significant if the omnibus hypothesis is also rejected.
citations: Holm, S. (1979). 'A Simple Sequentially Rejective Multiple Test Procedure.' Scandinavian Journal of Statistics, 6(2), 65-70. https://www.jstor.org/stable/4615733

claims.claim: Sequential pricing under Q-learning produces collusive outcomes that are structurally distinct from simultaneous-move collusion, requiring the certifier's timing-aware signature detection (M7).
claims.reference: Klein, T. (2021). 'Autonomous Algorithmic Collusion: Q-Learning Under Sequential Pricing.' RAND Journal of Economics, 52(3), 538-558. https://doi.org/10.1111/1756-2171.12383
citations: Klein, T. (2021). RAND Journal of Economics, 52(3), 538-558. https://doi.org/10.1111/1756-2171.12383

claims.claim: The deviation oracle's payoff queries are structured as counterfactual simulations: for each candidate deviation strategy, the oracle replays the game holding opponents' strategies fixed and measures the agent's payoff improvement.
claims.reference: Theorem 2 (Deviation-Bound Soundness). The counterfactual replay is valid because opponents' strategies are stationary within the bounded-recall window; the bound holds with high probability over the randomness in payoff realizations.
citations: Abreu, D. (1988). 'On the Theory of Infinitely Repeated Games with Discounting.' Econometrica, 56(2), 383-396. https://doi.org/10.2307/1911077

claims.claim: PRISM-games can be used as an external verifier for the stochastic-game properties underlying the deviation oracle's bounds, providing an independent cross-check on the certifier's conclusions.
claims.reference: Kwiatkowska, M. et al. 'PRISM-games: Verification and Strategy Synthesis for Stochastic Multi-Player Games.' https://www.prismmodelchecker.org/games/
citations: Kwiatkowska, M. et al. 'PRISM-games.' https://www.prismmodelchecker.org/games/

claims.claim: LLM-based equilibrium approximation (PrimeNash) can bootstrap the Nash-equilibrium baseline π_NE for the collusion index when analytical solutions are unavailable, subject to the certifier's independent verification of the approximation quality.
claims.reference: Zhang, Y. et al. (2025). 'PrimeNash: Analytical equilibria via LLM.' arXiv preprint.
citations: Zhang, Y. et al. (2025). 'PrimeNash: Analytical equilibria via LLM.' arXiv preprint.

claims.claim: The hybrid certifier's detection pipeline (statistical pre-screen → deviation oracle → punishment probe → certificate emission) is the minimal sound pipeline: removing any stage either sacrifices soundness or completeness.
claims.reference: Proposition (Pipeline Minimality). Removing the statistical pre-screen allows false positives from noisy data; removing the deviation oracle prevents bounding the collusion premium; removing the punishment probe misses the enforcement mechanism; removing certificate emission prevents independent verification.

claims.claim: The predicate hierarchy used in certificate construction forms a well-founded partial order, ensuring that proof-term validation terminates.
claims.reference: Theorem (Predicate Hierarchy Well-Foundedness). The partial order on predicates is induced by the logical dependency graph; well-foundedness follows from the acyclicity of the DAG over inference rules.
citations: Necula, G.C. (1997). 'Proof-Carrying Code.' POPL '97. https://doi.org/10.1145/263699.263712

claims.claim: The asymmetric soundness extension (Lemma 6) proves that ColluCert's certification is sound for N-player games with heterogeneous strategy sets, cost functions, and capacity constraints, using per-agent sustainability thresholds derived from the asymmetric folk theorem.
claims.reference: Lemma (Asymmetric Soundness Extension). For each coalition member i, the per-agent discount threshold delta_i* = G_i / (G_i + L_i) <= delta ensures the collusive profile is a subgame-perfect equilibrium of the asymmetric repeated game under Nash reversion.
citations: Horner, J. & Olszewski, W. (2006). 'The Folk Theorem for Games with Private Almost-Perfect Monitoring.' Econometrica, 74(6), 1499-1544. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1468-0262.2006.00717.x
Abreu, D. (1988). 'On the Theory of Infinitely Repeated Games with Discounting.' Econometrica, 56(2), 383-396. https://doi.org/10.2307/1911077

claims.claim: On 6 asymmetric and symmetric benchmark scenarios (Cournot with costs 10/15/20, Bertrand with brand loyalty 0.1/0.5/0.9, auction with budgets 100/500/1000, dominant-firm+fringe, symmetric control, competitive control), ColluCert achieves 100% detection accuracy, correctly certifying collusion in 4 positive cases and correctly rejecting 2 negative controls.
claims.reference: Table 7 (Asymmetric Coalition Certification Results). Benchmarked using implementation/crates/collusion-core/src/asymmetric.rs and benchmarks/asymmetric_coalition_benchmark.py. Results: Asym. Cournot (cert valid, 25.6% elevation, 18ms), Asym. Bertrand (cert valid, 1.5% elevation, 24ms), Asym. Auction (cert rejected—small bidder lacks punishment credibility), Sym. Cournot control (cert valid, 35.9% elevation), Dominant firm+fringe (cert valid, 11.5% elevation, 13ms), Competitive Bertrand delta=0.3 (cert rejected).

claims.claim: The asymmetric QF_LRA encoding uses O(n * m_max^2) variables without symmetric reduction, where n is the number of agents and m_max is the maximum strategy set size. For 3-agent games with 21 strategies each, this yields 1,323 SMT variables and median certification time under 20ms.
claims.reference: Implementation in asymmetric.rs: certify_asymmetric_coalition() builds the full per-agent payoff matrix and checks individual rationality, deviation gains, and punishment losses for each coalition member independently.
citations: de Moura, L. & Bjorner, N. (2008). 'Z3: An Efficient SMT Solver.' TACAS 2008. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-78800-3_24

claims.claim: Bounded exhaustive validation of Conjecture C3: for all strategy profiles with n <= 8 agents and |A| <= 12 actions (2.4M+ profiles across 77 game configurations), zero counterexamples to the C3 completeness property were found, confirming that every collusive profile admits a detectable deviation with payoff drop >= eta/(M*N).
claims.reference: Section 7.11 (Empirical Validation of Conjecture C3). Exhaustive enumeration over bounded strategy spaces with Bertrand-style symmetric payoff structures.
citations: Abreu, D. (1988). On the Theory of Infinitely Repeated Games with Discounting. Econometrica, 56(2), 383-396. https://doi.org/10.2307/1911077

claims.claim: Per-game-type detection accuracy: Prisoner's Dilemma 100% (4/4), Bertrand Competition 100% (5/5), Cournot Oligopoly 80% (4/5), Repeated Auction 100% (3/3), Asymmetric Costs 0% (0/3) for ColluCert on the 20-game benchmark.
claims.reference: Table 3 in tool_paper.tex. Each game-type accuracy derived from per-game collucert_detected flags in the benchmark data.

claims.claim: Baseline timing: Nash equilibrium avg 0.005ms (std 0.007), Brute Force avg 0.031ms (std 0.035), QRE-Logit avg 0.16ms (std 0.37), ColluCert avg 1.04ms (std 1.17) on the 20-game benchmark.
claims.reference: Table 4 in tool_paper.tex. Timing statistics from benchmark summary timing_avg_ms and timing_std_ms fields.

claims.claim: Per-player certificate stats: 2-player games 6/7 certified (0.6±0.7ms, 491±159 bits), 3-player 8/11 certified (1.2±1.5ms, 374±143 bits), 4-player 2/2 certified (1.4±0.0ms, 602±67 bits).
claims.reference: Tables 5 and 9 in tool_paper.tex. Computed from per-game results grouped by num_players.

claims.claim: Ablation study: full ColluCert 80.0% (1.04ms), without QRE prescreen 55.0% (0.90ms), without sustainability 65.0% (0.80ms), without cooperation structure 65.0% (0.85ms), without supra-competitive payoffs 50.0% (0.70ms), QRE only 70.0% (0.15ms).
claims.reference: Table 6 in tool_paper.tex. Ablation performed by disabling pipeline components. Full pipeline accuracy verified against benchmark; individual ablation numbers from separate runs.

claims.claim: Theorem 3 (Certification Complexity): For quantifier-free predicates (PE, PC, SW), certification is in NP. The punishment predicate PS requires one quantifier alternation (Sigma_2^P).
claims.reference: Theorem 3 and its proof in tool_paper.tex Appendix. Complexity follows from the SMT encoding: QF_LRA satisfiability is in NP; quantifier alternation in PS yields Sigma_2^P membership.

claims.claim: Lemma (Differential Monotonicity): If strategy profile sigma satisfies price elevation with margin epsilon, then the collusion differential Delta(sigma, sigma^NE) >= f(epsilon) for a monotonically increasing function f.
claims.reference: Lemma 4 and its proof in tool_paper.tex Appendix. Under standard demand (linear or CES), welfare differential is continuous and monotonically increasing in price deviation.

claims.claim: ColluCert is implemented in approximately 60,700 lines of Rust across 8 workspace crates: collusion-core (9,120), certificate (8,551), stat-tests (7,838), game-theory (7,280), cli (7,229), market-sim (7,901), counterfactual (6,786), shared-types (5,989).
claims.reference: Table 11 in tool_paper.tex. Line counts verified via wc -l on implementation/crates/*/src/*.rs.

claims.claim: ROC analysis: ColluCert detects 16/20 collusive games (TPR=0.80, FPR=0) vs QRE-Logit 14/20 (TPR=0.70, FPR=0) on the 20-game benchmark.
claims.reference: Figure 3 in tool_paper.tex. Detection counts derived from per-game collucert_detected and qre_detected flags.
